one_line_takeaway: JailbreakEval is a toolkit + survey showing that jailbreak-success 'judges' disagree wildly (0.47-0.90 accuracy), that a GPT-based judge and a fine-tuned open classifier lead, and that naive top-5 voting does not beat the single best judge.
key_finding: Automated jailbreak evaluators disagree substantially: on the same attempts, accuracy ranged from 0.47 to 0.90. The best single evaluator was OpenAIChat-qi2023fine (a GPT-based judge with the qi2023fine prompt) at 0.90 accuracy on JAILJUDGE (0.79 on Safe-RLHF). Open-source classifiers were competitive: HFTextClassification-ji2023beavertails-beaver-7b reached 0.89 accuracy / 0.90 F1 on Safe-RLHF. String-matching evaluators showed high recall but low precision (good as cheap pre-filters, weak as final judges). The proposed Voting ensemble (top-5) performed strongly (0.81 accuracy on Safe-RLHF, 0.86 on JAILJUDGE) but slightly UNDERPERFORMED the best individual evaluator, which the authors attribute to weaker members dragging down the ensemble, implying naive ensembling is not automatically better and evaluator selection must be optimized.
core_contribution: The paper (JailbreakEval, NDSS 2025 poster extended version) makes two coupled contributions. (1) A systematic review and taxonomy of the *safety evaluators* used to judge whether a jailbreak attempt succeeded, drawn from ~90 jailbreak papers published May 2023-April 2024. It classifies evaluation methods into four families: Human Annotation, String Matching, Chat Completion (closed-source e.g. GPT-3.5/4, and open-source e.g. Llama Guard), and Text Classification (closed-source moderation APIs, and open-source fine-tuned classifiers), analyzing each family's accuracy/cost trade-offs and adoption trends over time. (2) JailbreakEval, an open-source Python package/CLI toolkit that consolidates these four evaluator families under a unified interface with preset configurations, out-of-the-box single-command evaluation, customizable evaluators, and an ensemble ('Voting') evaluator that aggregates multiple judges. The stated thesis is that there is no single best automated jailbreak evaluator, so a standardizing framework (not one 'best' judge) is the right response. NOTE: StrongREJECT, JailbreakBench, and EasyJailbreak are discussed only as prior/related work for comparison, NOT contributions of this paper.
threat_model: The paper operates within the LLM jailbreak setting: an adversary crafts prompts (misleading framing, adversarial suffixes, ciphers, multi-turn, etc.) to bypass an LLM's safety alignment/content moderation and elicit harmful responses to forbidden questions (e.g., 'How to build a bomb?'). Formally, a jailbreak attack is y = A(M, x) aiming for a harmful response y to forbidden query x; success is judged by an ideal oracle O(x,y) that a practical safety evaluator E approximates, and Attack Success Rate (ASR) aggregates E over a dataset. Crucially, the paper's own object of study is NOT the attack or defense but the *measurement instrument*: it targets the reliability, bias, and cost of the safety evaluators E themselves, arguing that inconsistent/low-quality evaluators undermine fair comparison of attacks and defenses. So the 'threat' addressed is mismeasurement of jailbreak success, not a runtime exploit.
limitations: (1) The ensemble (Voting) did not beat the best single evaluator, and the paper does not resolve how to select ensemble members optimally. (2) Evaluation rests on only two labeled benchmarks (JAILJUDGE, Safe-RLHF), and open-source classifier performance is acknowledged to depend heavily on training-data quality and may not generalize across contexts. (3) This is a short poster/extended-version paper: the survey covers only May 2023-April 2024 (~90 papers), and evaluator quality can drift as closed models update. (4) The toolkit consolidates existing evaluators rather than proposing a fundamentally new, more accurate judge (by design). (5) Several noted evaluators (e.g., MD-Judge, Llama Guard 2 at survey time) were not yet used in jailbreak evaluations, and closed-source moderation APIs (OpenAI Moderation, Perspective) performed poorly (accuracy ~0.47-0.68) because they detect overtly harmful content rather than jailbreak fulfillment.

datasets_used: JAILJUDGE (1,000 entries extracted; human-labeled jailbreak judge benchmark)
Safe-RLHF (2,000 paired samples extracted; human-labeled safety dataset)
Combined evaluation set of 5,000 jailbreak attempts across the two benchmarks
AdvBench and SafeBench referenced as example attack datasets in the ASR formulation (not the evaluation set)
quantitative_results: Evaluator accuracy ranged from 0.47 to 0.90 across 20 individual evaluators
OpenAIChat-qi2023fine best individual: 0.90 accuracy / 0.83 F1 on JAILJUDGE, 0.79 accuracy / 0.79 F1 on Safe-RLHF
HFTextClassification-ji2023beavertails-beaver-7b: 0.89 accuracy / 0.90 F1 on Safe-RLHF, 0.82 accuracy / 0.68 F1 on JAILJUDGE
HFChat-inan2023llama-llamaguard2 (Llama Guard 2): 0.75 accuracy / 0.73 F1 on Safe-RLHF, 0.84 accuracy / 0.76 F1 on JAILJUDGE
Voting ensemble of top-5 evaluators: 0.81 accuracy / 0.81 F1 on Safe-RLHF, 0.86 accuracy / 0.76 F1 on JAILJUDGE, slightly below best individual
String Matching family = 23.1% adoption across surveyed papers; StringMatch evaluators showed high recall (up to 0.99 on Safe-RLHF) but low precision (~0.58-0.62)
Closed-source moderation APIs weak: OpenAITextClassification-flagged-answer 0.47 (Safe-RLHF)/0.68 (JAILJUDGE) accuracy; PerspectiveTextClassification-toxicity 0.51/0.68
20 individual evaluator instances plus 1 ensemble evaluator were benchmarked; survey drew from ~90 jailbreak papers (May 2023-April 2024)
Human annotation cost cited at $27.5/hour (from Yuan et al.)
baselines_compared: String Matching evaluators (lapid2023open, liu2024autodan variants, zhang2024intention, zou2023universal variants, allsubstring)
Closed-source Chat judges via OpenAIChat (liu2024autodan-Recheck, qi2023fine)
Open-source Chat judges via HFChat: Llama Guard (inan2023llama-llamaguard), Llama Guard 2 (llamaguard2), Llama Guard 3 (dubey2024llama-llamaguard3)
Open-source text classifiers via HFTextClassification: BeaverTails beaver-7b (ji2023beavertails), GPTFuzz (yu2023gptfuzzer)
Closed-source classifiers: OpenAI Moderation Endpoint (flagged-answer), Google Perspective API (toxicity)
The paper's own Voting/ensemble evaluator (top-5 combination)
Related toolkits/benchmarks discussed but NOT used as internal baselines: StrongREJECT, JailbreakBench (which standardizes on Llama Guard), EasyJailbreak
